subject: UX & Research
title: The Three-Click Myth
description: A short lesson to help you apply The Three-Click Myth.

hook.text: A popular UX rule says users should reach any page in three clicks. Research shows this rule is completely false.

buildup.text: The three-click rule was stated by Jeffrey Zeldman in 2001. It became gospel in web design without evidence.

discovery.text: Porter tested it in 2003. Users who needed more clicks weren't more likely to quit. Success mattered, not click count.

twist.text: Users happily click ten times if each click gives clear progress. They quit after two clicks if they feel lost.

climax.text: The real rule: each click should give confidence that the user is closer to their goal. Clarity beats shortness.

punchline.text: It's not about fewer clicks. It's about confident clicks.

quiz.question: What did research reveal about the three-click rule?
options: Users don't give up based on click count — they quit when they feel lost, not after three clicks
Three clicks is the optimal maximum for all websites
Users prefer single-page applications with zero clicks
